summary: Charity Majors asks her audience for help answering specific questions about observability tooling for the second edition of her book, covering migrations, cost management, observability teams, and build-vs-buy decisions. She frames this as a follow-up to an earlier call for advice on software buying, which she argues is one of the highest-leverage activities a senior engineer can perform.
key_points: Vendor engineering is one of the highest leverage activities for staff+ engineers
Software buying decisions affect every engineer and every customer in the organization
Managing observability costs across the three pillars model remains a major challenge
Staff+ engineers can develop executive-level skills through vendor management while staying as individual contributors
The chapter on buying software is shaping up to be a highlight of the book's second edition
money_quotes: I've come to believe that vendor engineering is one of the subtlest and most powerful practical applications of deep subject matter expertise, and some of the highest leverage work an engineer can do.
How often do you get to make decisions that leverage the labor of hundreds or thousands of engineers per year, for fractions of pennies on the dollar?
It is one of the only ways a staff+ engineer can acquire and develop executive-level communication, strategy, and execution skills while remaining an individual contributor.
I watched him alter the trajectory of multiple other companies, none of which he was working for, buying from, or formally connected to in any way — in the space of a few conversations.
key_insight: Vendor engineering — the art of evaluating, buying, and managing software tools — is an underrated, high-leverage skill that lets staff+ engineers develop executive-level strategic thinking while remaining individual contributors.
